blocks.content: Ethan did not mean to draw her again.
That felt important to establish, even if he was the only one arguing the case.

He sat at his desk beneath the small lamp in his bedroom, sketchbook open, the finished park painting propped against the wall to dry. His room smelled faintly of paint, coffee, and laundry he had absolutely meant to fold three days ago.
His mother was at work again, another late shift at the hospital. She had left a note on the fridge reminding him to eat something that was not coffee. He had read it while drinking coffee.
Now the house was quiet.
Quiet enough for his thoughts to get loud.
He turned the pencil between his fingers.
The first sketch was only supposed to be the archway.
Then the bench.
Then the fountain.
Then, somehow, a figure standing beside it.
Red hair suggested in loose lines. One hand holding a ridiculous stuffed dinosaur. A smile he could not quite capture because every time he tried, it looked too simple.
Cassandra.
He knew her name now.
That made it worse.
Names made people harder to forget.
Ethan leaned back in his chair and rubbed both hands over his face.
You talked for maybe three minutes.
Barely even that.
This is how people become embarrassing poems.
He looked at the sketch again.
Still, something about the day had lodged itself under his skin.
Not just because she was beautiful.
She was.
Obviously.
Painfully.
But it was more than that.
It was the way she said things like she was tossing pebbles into deep water just to hear what echoed back.
Most good things aren't.
Artists are allowed to be hopeful.
Who said things like that to strangers?
Who walked into a moment and made it feel like it had been waiting for her?
Ethan picked up the pencil again.
He darkened the line of the archway. Softened the shape of her face. Left the eyes unfinished.
He always left eyes unfinished.
Eyes made drawings too honest.
His phone buzzed.
A message from Noah.
Bro. You alive or dramatically painting somewhere?
Both.
Concerning. Did you eat?
Ethan glanced at the empty coffee cup on his desk.
Define eat.
I'm coming over tomorrow. You need adult supervision.
We're the same age.
Spiritually I am your father.
Ethan smiled and set the phone down.
His gaze drifted back to the sketch.
He thought of Cassandra looking at the fountain.
Make it less sad.
He stood, crossed the room, and looked at the painting drying against the wall.
The fountain in the painting did look better than the real one. Not perfect. Not magically restored. Just warmer. Gentler. As if light had found it worth touching.
Ethan stared at it for a long moment.
Then he picked up a small brush and added one thin line of gold along the water.
Hopeful, maybe.
Or dishonest.
He wasn't sure yet.
